title: Burden of disease
subtitle: [Disability-Adjusted Life Years](#dod:dalys) (DALYs) per 100,000 individuals from all causes. DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life.
caption:
Burden of disease, 2021
Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 individuals from all causes. DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life.

The choropleth map displays the global distribution of the burden of disease in 2021, quantified as Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 individuals. The color scale ranges from light beige, representing 0 DALYs, to deep maroon, representing 60,000 DALYs, with intermediate bands at 10,000, 20,000, 30,000, 40,000, and 50,000. The highest values, falling in the 50,000 to 60,000 band, are concentrated in sub-Saharan Africa, specifically visible in countries such as the Central African Republic, Chad, and Somalia. A second tier of high burden, in the 40,000 to 50,000 range, covers much of the surrounding region, including Nigeria, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Mozambique. In contrast, the lowest values appear across the Americas, Western Europe, and Australia, where nations like the United States, Canada, France, and Australia fall into the 10,000 to 20,000 band. Russia and parts of Eastern Europe occupy the 30,000 to 40,000 band, while China and India sit in the 20,000 to 30,000 range. Greenland is marked with hatching to indicate no data is available. The map shows a distinct geographic gradient where the highest disease burden per capita is located in central and western Africa, while the lowest burden is distributed across the Americas, Europe, and Oceania.
